game_name: Zelda II: The Adventure of Link

design_philosophy.core_loop: Overworld navigation (top-down) → random encounter trigger → side-scrolling combat with experience gain → return to overworld with progression. Repeat across 6 palaces with boss encounters.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Hostile encounters on overworld map force transitions between exploration and action combat; resource management of lives/experience points creates save-or-lose-all stakes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Link on a dangerous 'most adventuresome quest yet' involving resurrection of Princess Zelda through traversing treacherous side-scrolling dungeons with magical powers
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical departure from original Zelda's top-down perspective by introducing mandatory side-scrolling combat with RPG progression (experience points, attribute leveling). Significantly increased difficulty compared to series. Introduces lives system and permadeath mechanics uncommon for later canonical Zelda games. Proximity-based continuation system creates strategic tension around revival locations.

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives with proximity-based continue mechanic. Losing all lives resets game; in Japanese version attribute levels reset to lowest of three (permanent in Western version)
difficulty.continues_system: Proximity continue system based on lives remaining; no traditional continue from checkpoint
difficulty.checkpointing: Save system records ability levels and experience points toward next level, but resets accumulated points to zero on save/load
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: players choose which attributes (Attack, Magic, Life) to level up with accumulated experience points, creating different difficulty curves based on specialization strategy

progression.upgrade_system: Experience-based attribute leveling system: Attack (sword damage), Magic (spell efficiency), and Life (damage reduction). Each attribute levels 0-7 above starting point. Maxing attribute converts further points to extra lives. Separate Heart and Magic Container acquisitions provide permanent increases.
progression.unlock_mechanism: 6 well-guarded palaces must be accessed sequentially; crystals placed in palace statues break seal on Triforce; side-scrolling dungeon completion gates progression to next palace
replayability_hooks: RPG-style attribute specialization creates varied build strategies
Experience point distribution choices impact difficulty curve
Overworld enemy encounters randomized (blob=easy, biped=hard, Fairy=heal)
Different attribute level distributions change combat viability

world_building_techniques: Introductory text establishes premise: Ganon destroyed, Zelda under sleeping spell, Triforce in sealed palace
NPC interaction with dialogue-based storytelling (increased NPC interaction compared to original)
Overworld map navigation establishes geography of Hyrule as dangerous interconnected realm
Palace design and hazard placement suggests environmental storytelling of dungeon purposes

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-based combat encounters tied to palace architecture and themes; bosses serve as gatekeepers for crystal placement progression

faction_indicators: Impa represents authority figure delivering quest mission
Fairy encounters suggest benevolent world elements amidst hostile overworld
danger_signals: Random overworld encounters (small blob vs. large biped sprite differentiation) signal threat escalation
Side-scrolling combat shift from overworld navigation creates environmental danger context
Death Mountain and palace locations establish hostile geography
environmental_storytelling: Overworld top-down view shows Hyrule as interconnected dangerous terrain with navigation constraints
Palace graphics and window designs (some unused) suggest architectural differentiation and dungeon identity
Spike hazards and broken wall remnants in palace design communicate environmental threat
NPC placement in overworld suggests settlement and safe zones within hostile map
